caveats: a: O*NET covers the upholsterer occupation, which can include manufacturing and automotive tasks outside the frozen lens.
a: The BLS occupation chart covers the broader repair industry rather than NAICS 811420 alone.
rho: Census BTOS spans all industries and firm sizes and measures AI use in any business function.
rho: No industry-specific AI implementation evidence was found.

e: Because the injected count is zero, the eligibility share has no current counted firms to which it can be applied and is especially uncertain.

d5: Employment can fall because of labor supply or productivity even when service demand is stable.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include households, interior designers, hospitality and commercial facilities, furniture dealers, and institutional owners. Revenue comes mainly from custom job quotes covering skilled labor, fabric and other materials, refinishing, frame repair, pickup and delivery, and sometimes repeat commercial refurbishment programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Automotive and trailer upholstery, museum-piece restoration classified as artistic work, furniture manufacturing, internal captive workshops, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent US firms providing reupholstery, refinishing, repair, and restoration of furniture for external household and commercial customers. The screened population is limited to recurring or repeat outsourced-service firms in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: O*NET describes upholsterer tasks, while BLS reports upholsterers alongside managers and office clerks in the broader Personal and Household Goods Repair and Maintenance industry. I map work-order intake, material calculation, pattern and cutting-plan support, records, estimates, customer design discussion, scheduling, and administration to AI exposure, while treating stripping, sewing, webbing, padding, frame work, refinishing, handling, and installation as largely unexposed. The broad occupation mix may overstate administrative exposure for craft-heavy 811420 shops, while the upholsterer task list may understate owner and office work.
caveats: O*NET covers the upholsterer occupation, which can include manufacturing and automotive tasks outside the frozen lens.
The BLS occupation chart covers the broader repair industry rather than NAICS 811420 alone.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-use study for NAICS 811420 separating craft labor, design and estimating, pickup and delivery, sales, scheduling, bookkeeping, and management would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: O*NET shows that the core work is skilled, tactile craft using hand tools, sewing, materials, inspection, and physical restoration. AI can assist a narrower layer of quoting, design visualization, work-order processing, materials planning, records, and customer communication.

d5.bridge: BLS projects upholsterer employment to decline from 22.7 thousand in 2024 to 22.3 thousand in 2034, a 1.8 percent decrease. That is an occupation projection spanning employers and uses beyond NAICS 811420, not constant-price demand for the current repair service basket. I translate it to modestly declining five-year base demand with a wider downside from low-cost furniture replacement and craft scarcity and upside from sustainability, premium furniture, and commercial refurbishment. Employment may understate demand if labor scarcity or productivity reduces headcount and may overstate it if manufacturing upholstery offsets weaker repair demand.
caveats: BLS upholsterer employment includes work outside NAICS 811420 and is not a direct demand measure.
Employment can fall because of labor supply or productivity even when service demand is stable.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted same-shop order volume and revenue for reupholstery and repair, furniture replacement prices, commercial refurbishment cycles, and repair-versus-replace decisions by customer segment would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: The occupation outlook is approximately flat to slightly down, while the service faces substitution from new furniture and a shrinking craft workforce. Premium restoration, sustainability preferences, and recurring hospitality or commercial refurbishment provide offsetting durability.

caveats: The injected lower-middle-market firm count is zero and is a frozen ESTIMATE from a size-distribution and margin bridge, not proof that no such firm exists.
Because the injected count is zero, the eligibility share has no current counted firms to which it can be applied and is especially uncertain.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census using revenue mix, repeat-customer share, owner production hours, customer concentration, normalized EBITDA, management depth, and ownership would directly resolve eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is fundamentally an external repair and restoration service, but a lower-middle-market firm must also have repeat demand, normalized EBITDA in the frozen band, transferable operations, and control-sale suitability. Bespoke artisan work, owner craftsmanship, project concentration, mixed manufacturing, and limited scale can make nominally classified firms ineligible.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $997.3M / sectoral output (T30) $2,019.5M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $119,743 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 811420); class <5: 2,546 firms, avg revenue/firm $212,002 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,178 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 494 firms, avg revenue/firm $709,949 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $111,107 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 163 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,477,025 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $231,154 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,877,061 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $606,760 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 0

o.bridge: O*NET shows that upholsterers inspect furniture, strip materials, replace padding and springs, sew, attach fabric, repair frames, refinish wood, and physically handle furniture. I map these tactile and accountable tasks to continued operator requirement, allowing a small loss to do-it-yourself work, product replacement, standardized cover solutions, or manufacturing substitution. The task evidence may overstate operator requirement if modular products displace custom work, and may understate it where valuable furniture and commercial standards demand skilled restoration.
caveats: O*NET includes some upholsterer work outside the NAICS lens, including manufacturing and automotive tasks.
The estimate applies to the current service basket and does not predict long-run product redesign.
o.change_evidence: Customer and dealer data on do-it-yourself substitution, fitted-cover or modular-replacement adoption, furniture disposal versus repair, and robotics use in bespoke repair would materially change this value.
o.rationale: The current service basket is intrinsically physical and bespoke. AI can support design and administration but cannot manipulate varied furniture, fabrics, springs, frames, finishes, and tools without an accountable local operator.

caveats: No direct evidence was found on AI-enabled cost-savings retention in this industry.
Pricing power likely differs sharply between bespoke residential restoration and standardized commercial refurbishment.
q.change_evidence: Job-level quotes, realized labor and material costs, win rates, repeat-customer repricing, and gross-margin cohorts before and after workflow automation, separated between residential custom and commercial repeat work, would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Custom quotes, differentiated workmanship, design advice, materials markup, and customer valuation of restoration can allow meaningful retention of implemented benefit. Competitive bids for standardized commercial work, transparent material costs, quote repricing, and faster-turnaround sharing limit full capture.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures AI adoption across US businesses, not implementation of exposed work in small reupholstery and furniture-repair firms. I use the broad adoption baseline and adjust for available quoting, visualization, communications, recordkeeping, and materials-planning tools, while discounting for very small craft businesses, unstructured jobs, weak software integration, and the need for human judgment. This may overstate fragmented artisan shops and understate larger commercial refurbishment operators with standardized jobs.
caveats: Census BTOS spans all industries and firm sizes and measures AI use in any business function.
No industry-specific AI implementation evidence was found.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of independent furniture repair and reupholstery firms measuring current software and AI use, planned workflow deployment, job standardization, exception rates, and realized labor hours avoided would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Some exposed office and pre-production workflows can be implemented within five years, but bespoke inputs, craft judgment, limited digital records, and small-firm change capacity constrain deployment.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners, not completed qualifying control transfers among eligible NAICS 811420 firms. I discount the broad intention rate more heavily for the craft dependence and limited transferability of bespoke owner-operated shops, plus failed, family, or internal transfers. This may understate sales if the industry's owners are older than average, or overstate qualifying sales because intentions and business continuity do not ensure a completed control transfer.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed deals.
The frozen dataset count of zero makes realized transfer opportunity absent unless the dataset bridge misses firms.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transaction outcomes, owner-age data, and succession interviews for scaled furniture repair and reupholstery businesses would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Succession pressure can create sales, but owner-specific craft skill and customer relationships make a completed, transferable control transaction less likely than a broad employer owner's stated intention.

principal_driver: Nearly all of the current service quantity still requires skilled physical craft and an accountable operator.
principal_weakness: The frozen dataset estimates zero lower-middle-market firms, and bespoke owner-dependent craft work can be difficult to scale or transfer.
